questions.question: Who among the following officials was responsible for maintaining law and order in a village during Sher Shah’s administration?
questions.solution: In Sher Shah Suri's highly organized administrative system, the Shiqqdar was the military officer in charge of a Shiqq (district), primarily responsible for maintaining law and order. The Muqaddam was the village headman responsible for local crime and tax, the Qazi handled justice, and the Fotedar acted as the treasurer.

questions.question: In Brahmadeya villages under the Pallavas, which caste predominantly managed the local Sabha assemblies?
questions.solution: Brahmadeya villages were land grants given to Brahmanas. These villages were managed by an assembly of Brahmana landowners known as the Sabha. The Sabha functioned through various committees (variyams) to oversee irrigation, gardens, and temples.

questions.question: During the period of medieval Indian literature, which language began to decline as the dominant medium for Buddhist literary expression and scholarship?
questions.solution: By the medieval period, Pali, which had been the primary language of the Theravada Buddhist canon and scholarship, began to decline in dominance in India. It was gradually replaced by Sanskrit in Mahayana Buddhism and eventually by local vernacular languages as Buddhism itself declined in the subcontinent.

questions.question: In which of the following years was Burhan-ul-Mulk appointed the Nazim of Awadh?
questions.solution: Saadat Khan, also known as Burhan-ul-Mulk, was appointed the Nazim (Governor) of Awadh in 1722 by the Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah. He eventually laid the foundation for the independent state of Awadh as Mughal central authority weakened.

questions.question: Sher Shah divided his empire into shiqqs, equivalent to which administrative division under the Mughals?
questions.solution: Sher Shah Suri divided his empire into administrative units called shiqqs. These were governed by a Shiqqdar-i-Shiqqdaran. These units were equivalent to the Sarkar (district level) found in the later Mughal administrative hierarchy under Akbar.

questions.question: Amir Khusrau is known for contributing to which two languages?
questions.solution: Amir Khusrau, the 'Parrot of India,' was a legendary poet and musician. He was a master of Persian (the court language) and is also credited with the development of Hindavi (a precursor to Urdu and Hindi), using it to write folk songs and riddles that connected with the common people.

questions.question: Who among the following Delhi sultans accidentally died during a game of polo in the year 1210?
questions.solution: Qutbuddin Aibak, the founder of the Slave Dynasty and the Delhi Sultanate, died in 1210 due to a fall from his horse while playing Chaugan (an early form of polo) in Lahore. He was succeeded by Iltutmish after a brief interval.

questions.question: Which among the following South Indian kingdoms is known for the conquest that crossed the river Ganga in the north?
questions.solution: The Chola King Rajendra I (Rajendra Chola) conducted a daring military expedition to Northern India. His army reached the banks of the Ganga, defeating the Pala King Mahipala. To commemorate this victory, he assumed the title Gangaikonda ('Conqueror of the Ganges') and built the city Gangaikondacholapuram.

questions.question: During the Chola rule, which of the following was the land donated to Jaina institutions?
questions.solution:
The Chola inscriptions mention various categories of land grants based on their purpose:

  • Pallichchhandam: Land donated to Jaina institutions.
  • Shalabhoga: Land for the maintenance of a school.
  • Vellanvagai: Land of non-Brahmana peasant proprietors.
  • Tirunamattukkani: Land gifted to temples.

questions.question: What was the capital of Awadh when it was established in 1722?
questions.solution: When Burhan-ul-Mulk established the autonomous state of Awadh in 1722, the initial capital was Faizabad. It remained the capital until 1775, when Asaf-ud-Daula shifted the seat of government to Lucknow.

questions.question: In which of the following years did Vasco da Gama land in Calicut on the Malabar Coast for the second time?
questions.solution: After his historic first voyage in 1498, Vasco da Gama returned to India for a second time in 1502. This time he came with a heavily armed fleet to establish Portuguese commercial dominance and retaliate against the Zamorin of Calicut for previous hostilities.

questions.question: Alauddin Khilji introduced the Dagh and Huliyah systems to control the army. What was Huliyah associated with?
questions.solution:
Alauddin Khilji introduced two key reforms to prevent corruption in the military:

  • Dagh: The branding of horses to prevent the substitution of good horses with inferior ones.
  • Huliyah: A descriptive roll (physical description) of each soldier to ensure that a soldier didn't send a proxy for inspections or battle.

questions.question: Yagnavarman, Sardulavarman and Anantavarman were the kings of which of the following kingdoms?
questions.solution: Yagnavarman, Sardulavarman, and Anantavarman were prominent rulers of the Maukhari dynasty. Based in Kannauj, the Maukharis rose to power after the decline of the Guptas and were frequently in conflict with the Later Guptas of Magadha.

questions.question: In the Gupta period, the term Udranga referred to ______.
questions.solution: In Gupta administration, Udranga was a type of land tax, likely a tax on permanent tenants. Another common land tax mentioned in inscriptions is Uparika, which was probably an extra tax levied on temporary tenants.

questions.question: Who among the following Mughal rulers is considered to be the last powerful Mughal ruler?
questions.solution: Aurangzeb (reigned 1658–1707) is considered the last powerful Mughal emperor. After his death, the empire faced a rapid decline due to weak successors, internal rebellions, and the rise of regional powers like the Marathas and Sikhs.

questions.question: During Shahjahan’s rule, how many mansabdars out of 8000 were higher ranking mansabdars who received 61.5% of the total estimated revenue of the empire as salaries for themselves and their troopers?
questions.solution: By the time of Shah Jahan, the Mansabdari system had become highly stratified. Out of approximately 8,000 mansabdars, a tiny elite of 445 high-ranking officials held the majority of the power, receiving 61.5% of the empire's total revenue as salary for themselves and their troops.

questions.question: Who was the first Portuguese governor in India, sent in 1505 to establish naval supremacy?
questions.solution: Francisco de Almeida was appointed as the first Portuguese Governor and Viceroy in India in 1505. He is famous for the 'Blue Water Policy,' which aimed at establishing Portuguese naval supremacy in the Indian Ocean rather than building a land-based empire.

questions.question: When was the Gurjara Pratihara dynasty founded by Nagabhatta I in the region of Malwa?
questions.solution: The Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty was founded by Nagabhatta I in the 8th century (around 730 CE) in the Malwa region. He became famous for resisting Arab invasions from Sindh, protecting the heartland of India.

questions.question: Among which of the following powers did the ‘Tripartite Struggle’ take place during the 8ᵗʰ and 9ᵗʰ centuries?
questions.solution: The Tripartite Struggle was a long-drawn conflict over the control of the strategic city of Kannauj. The three powers involved were the Palas of Bengal, the Pratiharas of North-Western India, and the Rashtrakutas of the Deccan.

questions.question: Which of the following dynasties was NOT involved in the struggle for the control of the prosperous city of Kannauj in northern India?
questions.solution: While the Palas, Pratiharas, and Rashtrakutas fought bitterly for Kannauj, the Pallavas were a South Indian power based in Kanchi (Tamil Nadu) and were NOT involved in this northern struggle.

questions.question: Rohilla chief, Najib-ud-Daula, played a key role in the ____.
questions.solution: Najib-ud-Daula, a powerful Rohilla chief, was a staunch ally of the Afghan invader Ahmad Shah Abdali. He played a key role in the Third Battle of Panipat (1761) by providing local support and intelligence to Abdali against the Marathas.

questions.question: What was the main source of revenue for the Pallava empire, used for maintaining their army and administrative expenses?
questions.solution: Like most ancient and medieval Indian empires, the land tax (often 1/6th of the produce) was the main source of revenue for the Pallava state. This income was essential for maintaining their sophisticated administration and their frequent wars with the Chalukyas and Pandyas.

questions.question: Abu’l Fazl was the court historian of which of the following rulers?
questions.solution: Abul Fazl was the celebrated court historian of Akbar. He authored the Akbar Nama (the official history of Akbar's reign) and its third volume, the Ain-i-Akbari, which details the Mughal administration.

questions.question: The Pallava kings adopted which title to reflect that their rule was guided by the principles of the Dharmashastras?
questions.solution: To emphasize their legitimacy and commitment to Brahmanical social order, Pallava kings often adopted the title Dharma-Maharaja. This signified that their governance was strictly aligned with the principles of the Dharmashastras.

questions.question: The five village committees (variyams) such as neernilai variyam and needhi variyam were a unique administrative feature of which of the following dynasties?
questions.solution: The Pandyas had a unique administrative system where village affairs were managed by five specific committees (variyams). These included the Neernilai Variyam (water bodies/irrigation) and Needhi Variyam (justice), ensuring efficient local self-governance.
